title: Bṛhad Atiyāra — Extended Confession of the Twelve Lay Vows' Transgressions
summary: This is the long, detailed confession in which a Jain lay practitioner (śrāvaka) reviews the twelve household vows and admits, one vow at a time, every way they may have broken or strained each one. It is recited aloud as the heart of the fourth 'obligation' of pratikramaṇa — the act of 'turning back' from wrongdoing. Each section names the formal transgressions (atichāra) of a vow, lists concrete everyday lapses, and closes by sweeping them all up with the refrain 'for all this, by mind, speech and body, may my misdeed be in vain' (micchā mi dukkaḍaṁ). It is a tool of honest self-examination, not a fixed creed: the practitioner uses it to recall and release the faults of the period under review.
ritualPlacement: This is the **fourth Āvaśyaka** (Paḍikkamaṇa — reflection and atonement) in its **extended (bṛhad) form**. In the daily Devasi and Rāi pratikramaṇas this slot is filled by the **short** Laghu Atiyāra; in the longer **Pākṣika (Pakkhī, fortnightly), Cāturmāsika (Cāumāsī, season-end) and Sāṁvatsarika (Saṁvatsarī, annual)** pratikramaṇas it is replaced by this extended confession. Because, in this Anchalgachchha (Vidhipaksha) tradition, the Cāumāsī and Sāṁvatsarī rites have **no independent text** — they are performed exactly as the Pakkhī with the word 'pakkhī' swapped for 'cāumāsī' or 'saṁvatsarī' and a longer kāyotsarga — this single confession serves all three of those observances. State this substitution rule once: the per-verse commentaries assume it.
authorAndSource: Composed in **Old/Medieval Gujarati prose** (with Prakrit vow-formulae embedded), the atichāra confession is a traditional, **anonymous liturgical compilation** standardized by the gaccha's ācāryas rather than the signed work of a single named poet; the text printed here follows the **Anchalgachchha / Vidhipaksha edition of *Panch Pratikraman Sutra — Vidhi Sahit***. Its doctrinal scaffolding — the five aṇuvrata, three guṇavrata, four śikṣāvrata and their atichāras, the fifteen karmādāna, and the final sallekhanā — derives from the classical śrāvaka-ācāra tradition (Umāsvāti's *Tattvārtha-sūtra* ch. 7 and the *Upāsakadaśāṅga*). No single individual author is attested for the Gujarati confession itself; see notes.
structureArc: The confession moves vow by vow in fixed order. (1) opens with the 'great transgressions' (moṭā atiyāra): the foundation of samyaktva (right faith), its four-fold mithyātva and five atichāras (śaṅkā, kāṅkṣā, vitigicchā, para-pāṣaṇḍi-praśaṁsā, para-pāṣaṇḍi-saṁstava), the avoidance of folk festivals and tīrthas, then the **1st aṇuvrata, Prāṇātipāta-viramaṇa** (non-violence) with its five atichāras. (2) the **2nd aṇuvrata, Mṛṣāvāda-viramaṇa** (truthfulness); (3) the **3rd, Adattādāna-viramaṇa** (non-stealing); (4) the **4th, Śīlavrata / svadāra-santoṣa** (chastity); (5) the **5th, Parigraha-parimāṇa** (limiting possessions) — completing the five aṇuvratas. (6) the **6th vow, Diśivrata** (the first guṇavrata, directional limits); (7) the **7th, Bhogopabhoga** (the second guṇavrata, food-and-use), which embeds the **22 abhakṣya** and **32 anantakāya** lists; (8) the **fifteen karmādāna** (forbidden livelihoods) followed by the **8th vow, Anarthadaṇḍa-viramaṇa** (the third guṇavrata, avoiding purposeless harm). Then the four śikṣāvratas: (9) **Sāmāyika**, (10) **Deśāvagāśika**, (11) **Pauṣadha**, (12) **Atithi-saṁvibhāga**. (14) closes with the five atichāras of **apaścima maraṇāntika sallekhanā** (the final fast), the grand tally of **eighty-five (pañcyāśī) atichāras**, the confession of faults in the **five ācāras** (jñāna, darśana, [cāritra], tapa, vīrya), and the sealing gāthā 'Paḍisiddhāṇaṁ karaṇe…' with the Deśāvagāśika pratyākhyāna.
recurringImagery: This sūtra works not by metaphor but by a repeated **liturgical formula**, which should be understood once and then assumed at every verse. For each vow the pattern is: (a) name and define the vow and the practitioner's specific niyama (restriction); (b) announce 'I now purify its five atichāras' (… atiyāra śodhuṁ); (c) recite the Prakrit names of those atichāras; (d) gloss each with a litany of concrete, vividly domestic lapses (mistreating cattle, false weights, eating after dark, careless sweeping, idle quarrels); (e) gather everything — named and unnamed, gross and subtle ('sūkṣma, bādara'), known and unknown — and dissolve it in the closing refrain 'aneruṁ … je koi atiyāra huo hoy, te savi huṁ mane, vacane, kāyāe karī **micchā mi dukkaḍaṁ**' ('whatever further transgression there may have been, for all of it, by mind, speech and body, may my misdeed come to nothing'). The triad **mind–speech–body (mana–vacana–kāyā)** and the pairing **gross–subtle (sthūla–sūkṣma)** recur throughout as the axes along which every fault is measured.
keyThemes: Right faith (samyaktva) as the root that the vows grow from
The twelve lay vows: five aṇuvrata, three guṇavrata, four śikṣāvrata
Atichāra — transgression as strain or partial breach, distinct from outright abandonment
Exhaustive, concrete self-examination of everyday conduct
Confession by mind, speech and body; gross and subtle faults alike
Right livelihood: the fifteen forbidden trades (karmādāna)
Dietary restraint: the 22 abhakṣya and 32 anantakāya
The final fast (sallekhanā) and freedom from longing at death
Conduct in knowledge, faith, character, austerity and energy (the five ācāras)

glossary.definition: The ritual of 'turning back' — reflection, confession, atonement and renewal that returns a practitioner from transgression to right conduct.
A transgression of a vow: a partial breach, slackening or 'overstepping' that strains the vow without fully abandoning it. (Note this book spells it અતિયાર, with ya.)
A Jain lay follower who takes the householder's vows, as opposed to a mendicant (sādhu).
The five 'lesser vows' of the layperson: non-violence, truthfulness, non-stealing, chastity, and limiting possessions.
The three 'merit-enhancing vows' that reinforce the aṇuvratas: directional limits, limits on use/enjoyment, and avoidance of purposeless harm.
The four 'disciplinary vows' of spiritual training: sāmāyika, deśāvagāśika, pauṣadha and atithi-saṁvibhāga.
Right faith or right view — correct trust in the deva, guru and dharma — treated here as the root condition of all the vows.
Wrong belief; the opposite of samyaktva. The text lists a four-fold mithyātva (worldly/transcendent, regarding deva and guru).
'May my misdeed be in vain / come to nothing' — the Prakrit formula of repentance that seals each confession.
The fifteen occupations/trades a conscientious layperson avoids because they entail heavy harm to life (e.g. trades in fire, forests, ivory, poison, animals).
Foods forbidden to eat; the text enumerates the standard twenty-two (e.g. the five udumbara fruits, honey, butter, items 'gone bad in taste').
Bodies that house infinitely many souls (mainly tubers and roots); the text lists thirty-two such items to be avoided as food.
The voluntary, peaceful fast unto death undertaken with equanimity at life's end; its 'final' (apaścima) form has its own five atichāras of lingering desire.
Disciplined bodily stillness/meditation, measured in Logassa-counts, which frames the act of confession in the surrounding ritual.
Right conduct, here in five domains — knowledge (jñāna), faith (darśana), character (cāritra), austerity (tapa) and energy (vīrya) — whose lapses are confessed after the twelve vows.

howToRead: A newcomer should read it as a structured checklist of conscience: take each vow's plain definition and its closing micchā-mi-dukkaḍaṁ refrain, and let the dense lists of examples wash over you as illustrations rather than items to memorize. An advanced practitioner can dwell on the precise Prakrit atichāra-names, the karmādāna and abhakṣya/anantakāya enumerations, and the gross/subtle and mind–speech–body distinctions, using the confession as a fine instrument of self-audit for the fortnight, season or year being reviewed.

sources.title: Panch Pratikraman Sutra — Vidhi Sahit (Anchalgachchha / Vidhipaksha edition)
sources.consultedFor: The verbatim sūtra text, ritual placement and the Pakkhī/Cāumāsī/Sāṁvatsarī substitution rule (the authoritative source book of this tradition).

sources.title: Tattvārtha-sūtra of Umāsvāti, Chapter 7 (vows and their transgressions)
sources.consultedFor: The classical framework of the five aṇuvrata, three guṇavrata, four śikṣāvrata and the five atichāras of each vow and of sallekhanā.

sources.title: Anuvrata and the twelve vows of the Jain householder
sources.consultedFor: Confirming the categorization and count of the lay vows and their standard transgressions.

notes: The book's text fixes the total at 'pañcyāśī' (eighty-five) atichāras — counted as 5 of samyaktva + 25 of the five aṇuvratas + 15 of the karmādāna + the guṇavrata/śikṣāvrata atichāras + 5 of sallekhanā; general reference works often cite the bare figure as 84 or 124 under different counting conventions, so the in-text total of 85 is honored here as authoritative for this edition. The Gujarati confession is treated as an anonymous, traditionally standardized liturgical text; no single named author is securely attested, and any specific attribution should be flagged as uncertain rather than asserted. The verse numbering jumps from (12) to (14): in the printed sequence the sallekhanā section carries number (14), and the five-ācāra confession and sealing gāthās follow it — the per-verse commentaries should not infer a 'missing' independent verse 13. Per this edition's orthography, 'atichāra' is written અતિયાર (with ય) throughout; spelling and mātrā variants in the Prakrit formulae are the book's own and are preserved verbatim.
